text: The surgeon identifies the sacral promontory as the anatomical starting point for accessing the pelvic cavity during the hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon navigates towards the barorectal space during the laparoscopic radical hysterectomy procedure.
The surgeon identifies the medial ureter and the lateral external iliac vessel, highlighting the psoas muscle and the external iliac vessel in the surgical field.

The surgeon attempts a lymphadenectomy, focusing on opening the barorectal space, which is visually connected to the parapsychological space. The ureter is highlighted as an important anatomical structure in this process.
The surgeon identifies the uterine artery within the transverse section of the barrectal space, highlighting its anatomical position.

The surgeon highlights the anatomical relationship between the ureter and the uterine artery, noting the deep uterine veins situated above the uterine artery. This visualization is crucial for identifying potential sources of bleeding during the radical hysterectomy.
The surgeon performs lymphadenectomy, carefully dissecting lymphatic tissue in the pelvic region, ensuring the procedure is parallel to the external iliac vessel.

The surgeon identifies the ureter, highlighting its importance in the surgical field. The ureter is visible with its mesentery, and the surgeon ensures to stay away from the mesenteric subline to prevent postoperative hydroureter.
The surgeon identifies the lymphatic pathways running along the veins and ligaments, including the cervix, iliac nodes, uterus, paravortic nodes, ovaries, and lower third of the inguinal nodes.
The surgeon begins the pelvic lymphadenectomy, focusing on the area around the iliopterator nerve, in the context of treating endometriosis carcinoma.
The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery, noting its medial boundary, with the genitofemoral nerve positioned laterally, both situated on the underlying muscles.
The surgeon begins dissection of the bladder, focusing on its anatomical significance within the surgical field.
The surgeon approaches the fascia of the rectum, carefully avoiding the surrounding fat tissue that is visually associated with the rectum.
The surgeon positions the instruments on the posterior aspect of the uterus, focusing on the two layers of the rectum. The procedure begins with the creation of the colpotomy incision.
The surgeon uses a monopolar hook to perform a colpotomy, with the colpotomizer visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon performs a closure of the vaginal cuff using hexacorporeal sutures, demonstrating the technique visually.
The surgeon opens the pararectal space, followed by the paravesical space, with lateral traction entering the pelvis medially from the promontory.
The final shape of the pelvic lymphadenectomy is visible following the radical hysterectomy, with specimens prepared for histological examination.
